---
title: "1.3 Expressions and Output"
subtitle: "Using Objects and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Output and expressions

This topic connects two core skills:

::: {.tight-list}
- using `System.out.print` and `System.out.println` to display information
- evaluating <span class="term">arithmetic expressions</span> (算术表达式) using Java operators
:::

The main classroom habit is to predict output before running code.

## Output

### `print` versus `println`

Java has two common output methods:

::: {.tight-list}
- `System.out.println(value)` prints the value followed by a new line.
- `System.out.print(value)` prints the value without advancing to the next line.
:::

`"Hi there!"` is a <span class="term">string literal</span> (字符串字面量): zero or more characters enclosed in double quotes.

## Code Task

### Keep the output on one line

Run the starter code, then change it to print `Hi there!` on one line while keeping all three print statements.

```java
public class MyClass
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.print("Hi ");
        System.out.println("there");
        System.out.print("!");
    }
}
```

Target output:

```text
Hi there!
```

## Literals

### Fixed values in code

A <span class="term">literal</span> is the code representation of a fixed value.

::: {.tight-list}
- `"Hi there!"` is a string literal.
- `100` is an integer literal.
- `2.54` is a decimal numeric literal.
- `true` and `false` are boolean literals.
:::

Changing a literal in code changes the fixed value the program uses.

## Escape Sequences

### Special characters inside strings

Use a backslash when a character needs special treatment inside a string.

```java
public class TestEscape
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String message =
                "Here is a backslash quote \" "
                        + "and a backslashed backslash (\\) "
                        + "Backslash n \n prints out a new line.";
        System.out.println(message);
    }
}
```

AP CSA escape sequences here: `\"`, `\\`, and `\n`.

## Expressions and Operators

### One expression evaluates to one value

An <span class="term">arithmetic expression</span> combines numeric values, variables, and operators into one numerical result.

::: {.tight-list}
- `+` addition
- `-` subtraction
- `*` multiplication
- `/` division
- `%` remainder
:::

Java uses `*` for multiplication because keyboard character sets do not rely on a multiplication sign.

## Code Task

### Operators in action

Run the code. Predict every line first.

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(2 + 3);
        System.out.println(2 - 3);
        System.out.println(2 * 3);
        System.out.println(2 / 3);
    }
}
```

Starter output:

```text
5
-1
6
0
```

## Truncating Division

### `int / int` gives an `int`

An expression with only `int` values evaluates to an `int`. Integer division throws away the decimal part.

::: {.tight-list}
- `2 / 3` evaluates to `0`
- `2.0 / 3` evaluates to `0.6666666666666666`
- if at least one value is `double`, the expression evaluates to `double`
:::

Change the last line of the previous program to use `2.0 / 3`.

## Logic Error Task

### Centimeters to inches

This code compiles and runs, but it uses the wrong operation.

```java
public class CmToInches
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.print("100 centimeters in inches is: ");
        System.out.println(100 * 2.54);
    }
}
```

Fix the <span class="term">logic error</span>. One inch equals `2.54` centimeters, so the expression should divide by `2.54`.

## Divide by Zero

### A math error can become a run-time error

Dividing an integer by zero is undefined. In Java, attempting integer division by zero results in an <span class="term">ArithmeticException</span>.

```java
public class DivideByZero
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(3 / 0);
    }
}
```

This is a run-time failure, not a normal output value.

## Compound Expressions

### Operator precedence

Expressions can be combined into <span class="term">compound expressions</span> (复合表达式). Java uses precedence rules:

::: {.tight-list}
- parentheses first
- then `*`, `/`, `%`
- then `+`, `-`
- operators with the same precedence are evaluated left to right
:::

Extra parentheses are allowed when they make the intended grouping clearer.

## Code Task

### Predict before running

```java
public class TestCompound
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(2 + 3 * 2);
        System.out.println((2 + 3) * 2);
        System.out.println(2 + (3 * 2));
    }
}
```

Expected output:

```text
8
10
8
```

Explain why the first and third expressions match.

## Remainder Operator

### `%` gives the leftover part

The operator <span class="term">`%`</span> returns the remainder after integer division.

![Long division remainder](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-3-expressions-and-output/mod-py.png){fig-align="center" width="24%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- `5 % 2` evaluates to `1`
- `8 % 2` evaluates to `0`
- `3 % 4` evaluates to `3`
:::

With positive operands, the terms remainder and mod often give the same result for AP CSA work.

## Remainder Pattern

### Smaller left operand

If `x` is smaller than `y`, then `x % y` evaluates to `x`.

Reason:

::: {.tight-list}
- `y` goes into `x` zero times.
- The whole value of `x` is left over.
- Therefore `2 % 3` evaluates to `2`.
:::

Use this shortcut carefully only when both values are positive in AP-style questions.

## Code Task

### Remainder output

Predict all four lines, then check the result.

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(11 % 10);
        System.out.println(3 % 4);
        System.out.println(8 % 2);
        System.out.println(9 % 2);
    }
}
```

Expected output:

```text
1
3
0
1
```

## Quick Check

### Remainder questions

Answer each question and give the reason.

| Question | Answer | Reason |
|---|---:|---|
| `158 % 10` | `8` | dividing by `10` leaves the ones digit |
| `3 % 8` | `3` | `8` goes into `3` zero times |

The second question is a common trap because it is not asking for `8 % 3`.

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### Pay Calculator starter

![Dollar sign](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-3-expressions-and-output/dollarSign.png){fig-align="left" width="10%"}

Complete the expressions for a pay calculator.

```java
public class Challenge1_3_Pay_Calculator
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println("Pay for 4 hours of work at 10 dollars an hour");
        System.out.println(4  10);

        System.out.println("Number of hours worked for pay 120 dollars and rate 15 dollars per hour");
        System.out.println(120  15);

        System.out.println("Pay for 12 hours of work at 7.50 dollars an hour");
        System.out.println(        );
    }
}
```

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### Add the final two expressions

Extend the pay calculator with these two statements.

::: {.tight-list}
- print the integer number of hours worked if pay is `100` dollars and rate is `9` dollars per hour
- print the remainder when `100` dollars is divided by `9` dollars per hour
:::

Required expressions:

```java
4 * 10
120 / 15
12 * 7.50
100 / 9
100 % 9
```

The target output should contain `90.0` for `12 * 7.50`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- distinguish `System.out.print` and `System.out.println`
- define <span class="term">literal</span>, <span class="term">string literal</span>, and <span class="term">escape sequence</span>
- evaluate expressions using `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and `%`
- explain why `int / int` uses <span class="term">truncating division</span>
- identify a <span class="term">logic error</span> when the program runs but calculates the wrong result
- apply precedence rules to <span class="term">compound expressions</span>
- interpret `%` as the remainder after integer division
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
